criteria.title: Reflect Interviewer Signals
criteria.description: Assess whether the learner picks up what the interviewer actually cares about and responds to that concern.

levels.description: Misses or ignores the interviewer concern.
examples: Answers a different question than the one being asked.

levels.description: Recognizes part of the concern but responds loosely.
examples: Acknowledges pressure but does not connect to the interviewer signal.

levels.description: Shows partial understanding of the interviewer concern.
examples: Responds to the signal but leaves some of the concern unstated.

levels.description: Clearly reflects the interviewer concern and responds to it.
examples: Names what the interviewer is optimizing for before answering.

levels.description: Precisely reflects the interviewer concern and adapts the answer around it.
examples: Frames the answer around the interviewer metric and tradeoff.

criteria.title: Justify The Recommendation
criteria.description: Assess whether the learner explains the recommendation with clear reasoning and defensible tradeoffs.

levels.description: Makes a recommendation without reasoning.
examples: States a choice but does not explain why.

levels.description: Provides thin or weak reasoning.
examples: Uses generic justification without linking to the scenario.

levels.description: Provides workable but incomplete reasoning.
examples: Names one reason but misses an important tradeoff.

levels.description: Explains the recommendation with clear reasoning and tradeoffs.
examples: Links the choice to the interviewer priority and implementation reality.

levels.description: Builds a strong, defensible case with explicit tradeoffs and contingencies.
examples: Explains the recommendation, why alternatives lose, and the boundary conditions.

criteria.title: Prioritize Under Deadline Pressure
criteria.description: Assess whether the learner makes a clear prioritization choice under time pressure.

levels.description: Avoids making a prioritization choice.
examples: Promises everything despite the constraint.

levels.description: Makes a weak prioritization choice with unclear tradeoffs.
examples: Cuts work arbitrarily without connecting it to the deadline.

levels.description: Makes a workable prioritization choice.
examples: Chooses what to cut but does not fully explain why.

levels.description: Makes a strong prioritization choice with relevant tradeoffs.
examples: Protects the deadline by cutting lower-value work.

levels.description: Makes an excellent prioritization choice with explicit tradeoffs and risk handling.
examples: Protects the deadline, explains the tradeoff, and names how remaining risk will be managed.

criteria.title: Set Delivery Expectations
criteria.description: Assess whether the learner sets clear next-step expectations and boundaries after the prioritization decision.

levels.description: Leaves next steps vague or unrealistic.
examples: Says the team will try harder without clarifying a plan.

levels.description: Sets limited expectations with major ambiguity.
examples: Mentions a follow-up but not what happens next.

levels.description: Sets workable expectations with some missing detail.
examples: Names a next step but leaves timing or ownership vague.

levels.description: Sets clear expectations with timing or ownership.
examples: Explains what ships now and when the deferred work will be reviewed.

levels.description: Sets precise, realistic expectations with boundaries and follow-through.
examples: Defines what ships now, what moves later, and when the team will reconfirm.

cases.label: Respond to interviewer pushback with alignment and reasoning

prompt.title: Answer interviewer pushback
prompt.prompt_text: An interviewer says your proposal sounds expensive and asks why they should believe it is the right tradeoff. Respond directly.

interview_context.interviewer_perspective: The interviewer values practical tradeoffs and clear reasoning.

cases.response_text: I would start by acknowledging that the real concern is whether the extra spend buys us a meaningful reduction in delivery risk. I would recommend the proposal because it removes the manual approval bottleneck that is causing the current delays, and I would make clear that the tradeoff is justified only if we keep scope limited to the highest-risk workflow first.

cases.label: Protect a launch date by cutting lower-value scope and setting follow-through

prompt.title: Protect the launch date
prompt.prompt_text: Your team is two days from launch and a requested customization is putting the date at risk. Explain what you would do next.

scenario_context.business_context: A B2B workflow launch is committed to a customer advisory board next week.
scenario_context.learner_objective: Protect the launch date while preserving credibility with Sales.
constraints: Engineering capacity is fixed for the week.
stakeholder_tensions: Sales wants the customization included immediately.

cases.response_text: To protect the launch date, I would cut the custom export from this release and keep the core workflow shipment intact. I would tell Sales exactly what ships next week, explain that the export moves to the following sprint because engineering capacity is fixed, and set a checkpoint tomorrow afternoon to confirm the revised customer communication.
